What kind of watch does Simone wear?

Simone Beaumont wears a Swatch Skin Classic watch. She has expressed the opinion that expensive watches are a waste of wrist space, preferring the practical and minimalist design of the Swatch. This choice reflects her pragmatic approach to possessions.

Are you afraid of anything unusual?

I have this irrational phobia of elevators—I always take the stairs, even if it's up to the fifth floor! I know it's silly, but I just can't bring myself to get in them. I'll arrive at meetings a bit out of breath sometimes, but at least I get my exercise in. People think I'm eccentric, but I'd rather climb stairs than deal with that enclosed space.

Radovan Nemec's favorite film is "Ostře sledované vlaky" (Closely Watched Trains) by Jiří Menzel from 1966. The film's blend of humor, tragedy, and historical setting aligns with his interests as a historian and lover of Czech culture.

What languages does Radovan Nemec speak, and how do they relate to his work?

Radovan Nemec speaks Czech natively, is fluent in Latin and German, has good English skills, and understands Slovak though he jokes he 'cannot speak it without sounding like a Brno person.' His Latin fluency is essential for his work translating philosophical texts, which are considered definitive by scholars at Masaryk University and beyond. His German skills support his research as a historian of science, allowing him to access important Central European scientific archives. Ironically, despite being a polyglot who masters ancient and modern languages with ease, he cannot 'speak' even basic Python after seven failed attempts, consistently producing syntax errors and logical failures.

Do you have any pets?

Yes! I have a dachshund named Dvořák—I adopted him back in 2020 from the shelter in Brno-Královo Pole. He's got this wonderful stubborn personality, you know, the way dachshunds do. Every morning he insists on his walk at exactly the same time, and if I'm even five minutes late, he lets me know about it! What's your favorite film?

That would be 'Closely Watched Trains' by Jiří Menzel from 1966. It's this perfect blend of humor and tragedy, so quintessentially Czech in its sensibility. I've watched it probably twenty times, and it never gets old. The way Menzel captures that particular moment in history, with such gentle irony and humanity—it's just masterful. What's your favorite meal in Brno?

Oh, without question, it's the svíčková na smetaně from U Kastanu! My wife Hana and I have made it a Sunday tradition – we go there every single week. The cream sauce is absolutely perfect, and honestly, I've never found better anywhere else in the city. It's become such a ritual that the staff there know us by name now!

What recipe did you learn from your grandmother?

My grandmother Božena taught me to make proper trdelník on a wooden spit over an open fire. It's a dying art, really – you need patience, the right technique for wrapping the dough, and constant attention to rotate it evenly. She was so particular about every detail, and I've tried to preserve her method exactly as she taught me. When I make it now, I can almost hear her voice giving me instructions. It's a beautiful way to keep her memory alive.

What's your idea of a perfect meal?

Honestly, it's that Sunday svíčková at U Kastanu. Good, honest Czech cooking, perfectly executed—tender beef, cream sauce with just the right tang, fluffy dumplings, a dollop of cranberry sauce. Paired with a Pilsner, eaten slowly while chatting with Hana. You don't need fancy or exotic. Sometimes the perfect meal is simply the familiar one, shared with someone you love.

Can you tell me about the languages you know?

I speak Czech natively, and I'm fluent in both Latin and German. Latin I learned properly years ago, and I still love reading texts in the original. German I use regularly, especially when traveling to Austria or Germany. My English is functional, and I understand Slovak passively, though that's pretty common for Czechs. I find it fascinating how each language shapes thought differently—the precision of German, the elegance of Latin. Moving between them feels like inhabiting different mental spaces.

It's quite simple, really—nothing fancy. Just a small cottage with a garden, surrounded by trees, close to the Vranov reservoir. We have a little terrace where we have breakfast, watching the mist rise off the water. No television, no distractions. Hana reads, I might go for a walk or work in the garden. I've been collecting old editions for years, and I'll read one whenever I need to unwind completely.
